name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا جَعَلَكُم مُّسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧)
```

```english
Have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that of which He has made you heirs. There is a great reward for those of you who have faith and spend [in Allah’s way]. (7)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
```

```english
Why should you not have faith in Allah when the Apostle invites you to have faith in your Lord, and He has certainly made a covenant with you, if you are [genuinely] faithful? (8)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لِّيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩)
```

```english
It is He who sends down manifest signs to His servant that He may bring you out of darkness into light, and indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to you. (9)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوِى مِنكُم مَّنْ أَنفَقَ مِن قَبْلِ ٱلْفَتْحِ وَقَـٰتَلَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَقَـٰتَلُوا۟ ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١٠)
```

```english
Why should you not spend in the way of Allah, when to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth? Those of you who spent [their means] and fought before the victory are not equal [to others]. They are greater in rank than those who have spent and fought afterwards. Yet Allah has promised the best reward to each and Allah is well aware of what you do. (10)
```

```arabic
مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥ وَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (١١)
```

```english
Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, that He may multiply it for him and [that] there may be a noble reward for him? (11)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ تَرَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَسْعَىٰ نُورُهُم بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِم بُشْرَىٰكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٢)
```

```english
The day you will see the faithful, men and women, with their light moving swiftly in front of them and on their right, [and they are greeted with the words:] ‘There is good news for you today! Gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success.’ (12)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱنظُرُونَا نَقْتَبِسْ مِن نُّورِكُمْ قِيلَ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ وَرَآءَكُمْ فَٱلْتَمِسُوا۟ نُورًا فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُم بِسُورٍ لَّهُۥ بَابٌۢ بَاطِنُهُۥ فِيهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةُ وَظَـٰهِرُهُۥ مِن قِبَلِهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ (١٣)
```

```english
The day the hypocrites, men and women, will say to the faithful, ‘Please wait, that we may glean something from your light!’ They will be told: ‘Go back and grope for light!’ Then there will be set up between them a wall with a gate, with mercy within and punishment without. (13)
```

```arabic
يُنَادُونَهُمْ أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنتُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ وَٱرْتَبْتُمْ وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْأَمَانِىُّ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَغَرَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (١٤)
```

```english
They will call out to them, ‘Did we not use to be with you?’ They will say, ‘Yes! But you cast yourselves into perdition. You awaited and were skeptical, and [false] hopes deceived you until the edict of Allah came, and the Deceiver deceived you concerning Allah. (14)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ مَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ هِىَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٥)
```

```english
So today no ransom shall be taken from you or the faithless. The Fire will be your abode: it is your [ultimate] refuge and an evil destination.’ (15)
```

## (Commentary)

This is an emphatic command to spend in the way of Allah, specifically regarding struggle (الجهاد) — *the exertion of effort in the cause of Allah* — as supported by His saying: **لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ** (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought) [Qarai 57:10]. This is further supported by the view that His saying: **آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَأَنْفِقُوا** (Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend...) [Qarai 57:7] was revealed during the Expedition of Tabuk.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَأَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُمْ مُسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ** (Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that in which He has made you successors...) [Qarai 57:7]. What is understood from the context of the verses is that the address in this verse is to the believers in Allah and His Messenger, not to the disbelievers, nor to the believers and disbelievers together as has been suggested. The command to those who have already embraced faith (الإيمان) in Allah and His Messenger to "believe" means a command to realize the reality of faith by manifesting its practical effects. For if a quality such as generosity, chastity, or courage were truly established in a person's soul, its specific effect would never fail to manifest. Among the effects of faith in Allah and His Messenger is obedience to what Allah and His Messenger have commanded.

From here, two points become clear: First, the command to a believer to believe is, in reality, a command to one who has attained a certain level of faith to ascend to a higher level. This type of command contains an allusion (إيماء) that what the commanded person currently possesses of the commanded matter does not fully satisfy the Commander.

Second, His saying: **آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَأَنْفِقُوا** (Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend) is a command to spend while hinting that it is an effect of a quality they already possess; thus, they ought to spend because they are characterized by it. This effectively makes their faith the underlying cause (تعليل) for the spending.

Regarding His saying: **وَأَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُمْ مُسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ** (and spend out of that in which He has made you successors), the "succession" (استخلاف) of man means making him a successor (خليفة). This refers either to their succession from Allah, the Glorified, as they succeed Him on earth, as indicated by His saying: **إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** (Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth) [Qarai 2:30]. Describing the wealth in their hands with this expression serves to clarify the reality and to encourage them to spend. For if they are certain that the wealth belongs to Allah and that they are merely successors over it—deputies (وكلاء) on His behalf who dispose of it as He has permitted—it becomes easy for them to spend it, and their souls do not feel constrained by doing so.

Alternatively, it refers to their succession of the generations that preceded them, just as every generation succeeds the one before it. This expression also encourages spending; for if they remember that this wealth belonged to others and did not remain with them, they will realize that it likewise will not remain with them and that they will leave it for others. Thus, spending it becomes easy for them, and their souls become generous with it.

His saying: **فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَأَنْفَقُوا لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ** (So those of you who believe and spend shall have a great reward) [Qarai 57:7] is a promise of reward for spending to reinforce the encouragement. What is meant by faith here is faith in Allah and His Messenger.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِرَبِّكُمْ** (Why should you not believe in Allah while the Apostle invites you to believe in your Lord...) [Qarai 57:8]. What is meant by faith is faith such that its effects follow from it—among which is spending in the way of Allah. Or if you prefer, it means manifesting the effects of the faith they already possess.

His saying: **وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِرَبِّكُمْ** (while the Apostle invites you to believe in your Lord). He expressed the Lord as "the Lord" (الرب) and annexed it to them as an allusion to the reason for the invitation and the command. It is as if it were said: He invites you to believe in Allah because He is your Lord, and it is obligatory upon you to believe in Him.

His saying: **وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَاقَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** (and He has already taken a pledge from you, if you are [truly] believers) [Qarai 57:8] is a reinforcement of the rebuke understood from the beginning of the verse. The pronoun in "He has taken" (أخذ) refers either to Allah, the Glorified, or to the Messenger. In either case, the "pledge" (الميثاق) — *the solemn covenant of loyalty and obedience* — refers to what is indicated by their testimony to the oneness of Allah and the messengership of His Messenger on the day they believed in him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), signifying that they are committed to hearing and obeying.

It has been said that the pledge refers to the covenant taken from them in the realm of pre-existence (الذر). According to this, the pronoun in "He has taken" refers to Allah, the Glorified. However, this is far-fetched given the context of the argument against them, as they are heedless of it. Furthermore, the taking of the pledge in the pre-existent realm is not exclusive to the believers but encompasses the hypocrites and the disbelievers as well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ عَلى عَبْدِهِ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لِيُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ** (It is He who sends down manifest signs to His servant that He may bring you out of darkness into light...) [Qarai 57:9]. The "manifest signs" (الآيات البينات) refer to the verses of the Noble Quran which clarify for them the religious obligations (فرائض) incumbent upon them. The subject of "that He may bring you out" (ليخرجكم) is the pronoun referring to Allah or to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The ultimate reference of the second is also the first; for the pledge is His pledge, and He took it through the medium of His Messenger or without a medium, just as faith in Him and His Messenger is faith in Him. Therefore, He said at the beginning of the verse: **وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ** (Why should you not believe in Allah), mentioning Himself and not mentioning His Messenger, to indicate that faith in His Messenger is faith in Him.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ اللهَ بِكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ** (and indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to you) [Qarai 57:9]. Ending the verse with His kindness and mercy is an indication that the faith to which His Messenger invites them is for their own good and benefit; they are the ones who profit from it, not Allah or His Messenger. Thus, it contains a reinforcement of the encouragement toward faith and spending.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيراثُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Why should you not spend in the way of Allah, while to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth?...) [Qarai 57:10]. **Heritage** (الميراث) and **legacy** (التراث) refer to wealth that passes from the deceased to those who remain after him among his heirs. The annexation of "heritage" to "the heavens and the earth" is explanatory (بيانية); thus, the heavens and the earth are themselves the heritage, including all the things created from them that are possessed by the sentient beings (ذوو الشعور) inhabiting them. The heavens and the earth encompass everything within them that was created from them.

Sentient beings, such as humans, for example, dispose of these things by designating what they use for themselves, which is the conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) that Allah, the Glorified, guided them to conceptualize among themselves so that the aspects of their worldly life might be organized.

However, they do not remain, nor does what they possess remain for them; rather, the death decreed among them takes them away, and what was in their hands passes to those after them, and so on, until everyone perishes and none remains but He, the Glorified.

The earth, for instance, and what is in it and upon it of wealth, is a heritage in the sense that every generation of its inhabitants inherits it from the one before it; thus, it is a constant heritage circulating among them, successor after predecessor. It is also a heritage in the sense that they will all perish, and nothing will remain of it except for Allah, who made them successors over it.

To Allah, the Glorified, belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth in both senses. As for the first: because He is the one who grants them ownership of the wealth, and He is the true Owner of what He has given them to possess. The Exalted said: **لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth) [Qarai 31:26], and He said: **وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth) [Qarai 24:42], and He said: **وَآتُوهُمْ مِنْ مَالِ اللَّهِ الَّذِي آتَاكُمْ** (and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you) [Qarai 24:33].

As for the second sense: it is evident from the verses concerning the Resurrection, such as His saying: **كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ** (Everyone on it is ephemeral) [Qarai 55:26] and others. What first comes to mind is that the meaning of them being a "heritage" is this second sense.

In any case, the verse contains a severe rebuke for their failure to spend in the way of Allah from the wealth that, in reality, none shall inherit but He, the Exalted, and which shall remain neither for them nor for anyone else. The use of the explicit noun in place of a pronoun in His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ** (and to Allah) is to intensify the rebuke.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ أُولئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ بَعْدُ وَقَاتَلُوا** (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought. They are higher in rank than those who spent afterwards and fought...) [Qarai 57:10]. **Equality** (الاستواء) means being the same. The counterpart to His saying: "those who spent before the conquest and fought" is omitted for the sake of conciseness (إيجاز), as it is indicated by His saying: "They are higher in rank than those who spent afterwards and fought."

The "conquest" (الفتح) refers—as has been said—to the Conquest of Mecca or the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. The coupling of fighting (القتال) with spending (الإنفاق) is not devoid of an allusion, or rather a demonstration, that the spending in the way of Allah encouraged in these verses is spending for the sake of struggle (الجهاد).

It is as if the verse is intended to clarify that spending in the way of Allah, the more it is hastened toward, the more beloved it is to Allah and the greater its rank and station. Otherwise, it is evident that these verses were revealed after the conquest and the fighting that they rushed toward before the conquest, and after some of the battles that occurred after it.

His saying: **وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى** (and to each Allah has promised the best reward) [Qarai 57:10]; that is, Allah has promised the best reward to everyone who spent and fought before the conquest, and everyone who spent and fought after it, even though the first group is greater in rank than the second. In this, there is a consolation for the hearts of those who were delayed in spending and fighting, that they have a share of His mercy and are not absolutely deprived; thus, there is no reason for them to despair of it even if they were delayed.

His saying: **وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ** (and Allah is well aware of what you do) [Qarai 57:10] is a concluding remark (تذييل) related to all that preceded. It contains an intensification of the rebuke and a confirmation and establishment of His saying: "Not equal among you..." and His saying: "and to each Allah has promised the best reward." It is possible that it relates only to the last sentence, but its relation to the whole is more general and comprehensive.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً فَيُضاعِفَهُ لَهُ وَلَهُ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ** (Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, that He may multiply it for him, and [that] there may be a noble reward for him?) [Qarai 57:11]. Al-Raghib said: "That which is given to a person of wealth on the condition of returning its equivalent is called a **loan** (قرض)." [End quote]. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "Its root is 'cutting' (القطع); thus, it is a portion cut off from its owner with his permission on the guarantee of returning its like." He said: "**Multiplication** (المضاعفة) is an increase over the original amount by its like or many times its like." [End quote]. Al-Raghib said: "**Reward** (الأجر) and wages (الأجرة) refer to what returns as a recompense for an action, whether worldly or otherworldly." He said: "It is only used for benefit, not for harm, unlike 'recompense' (الجزاء), which is used for both benefit and harm." [End quote summary].

What the Exalted gives of reward for the servant's deed is a favor (تفضل) from Him, without any entitlement on the part of the servant. For the servant and the deeds he performs are the absolute property of Him, the Glorified—an ownership that does not accept transfer or transition. However, He has considered, by way of a legislative consideration (اعتبار تشريعي), the servant as an owner and his deed as his property—while He is the true Owner of what He has given him to possess, which is another favor. Then He chose what He loved of his deeds and promised him a reward for his deed, calling it "wages" (أجر) and "recompense" (جزاء), which is yet another favor. None benefits from this in this world or the hereafter except the servant. The Exalted said: **لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَاتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** (For those of them who are virtuous and God-conscious there shall be a great reward) [Qarai 3:172], and He said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ** (As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there is for them an undiminished reward) [Qarai 41:8], and He said after describing Paradise and its bliss: **إِنَّ هَذَا كَانَ لَكُمْ جَزَاءً وَكَانَ سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكُورًا** (This is indeed your reward, and your endeavor has been appreciated) [Qarai 76:22]. What He promised of appreciation (الشكر) and the absence of obligation (عدم المن) when granting the reward is the completion of the favor.

In the verse, there is an eloquent urging toward the encouraged spending in the way of Allah, as He asks who among them will spend in the way of Allah, likening his spending to a loan he gives to Allah, the Glorified, which He must return. Then He definitively states that He will not return its mere equivalent to him, but will multiply it. He did not stop there but added to it a "noble reward" (أجر كريم) in the hereafter. A noble reward is that which is pleasing in its kind, and the otherworldly reward is such because it is the ultimate conceivable blessing at the time of the ultimate conceivable need.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى نُورُهم بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ** (The day you will see the faithful men and the faithful women, with their light moving swiftly before them and on their right...) [Qarai 57:12]. "The day" (اليوم) is the adverbial qualifier for His saying: "there may be a noble reward for him," and it refers to the Day of Resurrection. The address in "you will see" (ترى) is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or to every listener capable of being addressed. It appears that the preposition "with" (بـ) in "on their right" (بأيمانهم) means "in."

The meaning is: For the one who lends Allah a good loan, there is a noble reward on the day when you, O Messenger—or anyone capable of seeing—will see the men and women who believe in Allah and His Messenger, their light (نور) — *the spiritual radiance resulting from faith and righteous deeds* — moving swiftly in front of them and in their right hands. The "right" (اليمين) is the direction from which their felicity (سعادة) comes.

The verse is general, encompassing the believers of all nations and is not restricted to this nation. Expressing the radiance of the light as "moving swiftly" (سعي) suggests that they are hastening toward the degrees of Paradise that Allah, the Glorified, has prepared for them. The directions of felicity and the stations of proximity (مقامات القرب) are illuminated for them one after another until their light is completed for them, as the Exalted said: **وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَرًا** (Those who were God-conscious of their Lord will be led to Paradise in throngs) [Qarai 39:73], and He said: **يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمَنِ وَفْدًا** (The day We shall muster the God-conscious toward the All-beneficent, like guests of honor) [Qarai 19:85], and He said: **يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِي اللَّهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا** (the day Allah will not let the Prophet down and those who have faith with him. Their light will move swiftly before them and on their right, while they say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us...’) [Qarai 66:8].

The commentators have various opinions regarding the interpretation of the individual words of the verse, which we have overlooked due to the lack of evidence from the wording of the verse itself. What is found in the transmitted narrations will be presented in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," if Allah wills.

His saying: **بُشْراكُمُ الْيَوْمَ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها** (‘Good news for you today! Gardens with conduits running amidst them, to remain in them [forever]...’) [Qarai 57:12] is a narration of what will be said to the believing men and women on the Day of Resurrection. The speakers are the angels by the command of Allah, and the implied meaning is: It will be said to them: "Your good news..." etc. What is meant by "good news" (البشرى) is that which is announced, which is Paradise, and the rest is clear.

His saying: **ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ** (...That is the great success) [Qarai 57:12] is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and the reference is to the aforementioned moving of the light and the good news. Or it is part of the completion of the angels' speech, and the reference is to the gardens and the eternal dwelling therein.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالْمُنافِقاتُ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا انْظُرُونا نَقْتَبِسْ مِنْ نُورِكُمْ** (The day the hypocrites, men and women, will say to the faithful, ‘Wait for us, so that we may take a light from your light!’...) [Qarai 57:13]. The verb "to look" (النظر), when it is transitive by itself, conveys the meaning of waiting and granting respite. When it is transitive with "toward" (إلى), it means casting one's sight toward something. When it is transitive with "in" (في), it means reflection. "Acquiring" (الاقتباس) is taking a flame (قبس) from a fire.

The context indicates that they are on that day in a darkness whose canopy has surrounded them, and they are forced to travel toward their eternal abode. However, the believing men and women travel by their light which moves swiftly before them and on their right; thus, they see the path and are guided to their stations. As for the hypocrites (المنافقون) — *those who outwardly profess faith while inwardly concealing disbelief* — they are covered in darkness, unable to find a way. They are with the believers just as they were with them in the world, counted among them. Then the believing men and women outpace them toward Paradise, while the hypocrites, men and women, fall behind in a darkness that covers them. They ask the believing men and women to wait for them so they may catch up and take a flame from their light to illuminate their way.

His saying: **قِيلَ ارْجِعُوا وَراءَكُمْ فَالْتَمِسُوا نُوراً** (It will be said, ‘Go back and look for a light!’...) [Qarai 57:13]. The speaker is either the angels or a group of the perfected believers, such as the People of the Elevations (أصحاب الأعراف).

In any case, it is from Allah and by His permission. The address: "Go back and look for a light!" has been said to be an address based on mockery and derision, just as they used to mock the believers in the world. More apparent, based on this, is that "back" (الوراء) refers to the world. The summary of the meaning is: Return to the world which you left behind your backs and in which you performed what you performed of hypocrisy, and seek light from those deeds; for light is only the light of deeds or faith, and you have neither faith nor deeds.

This can be considered a distinct interpretation without the meaning of mockery, such that His saying: "Go back" is a command to return to the world and acquire light through faith and righteous deeds, though they are not returning and are unable to do so. Thus, the command to return is like the command to prostrate mentioned in His saying: **يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ** (The day when the shank is bared, and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, but they will not be able [to do it]. Their looks will be cast down, and abasement will overcome them, for they were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet whole) [Qarai 68:42-43].

It has also been said: The meaning is, return to the place where the light was distributed and seek it from there. So they return but find nothing, and they turn back toward the believers, only to find that a wall has been set up between them. This is a deception from Him, the Exalted, by which He deceives them just as they used to deceive Him in the world, as He said: **إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ يُخادِعُونَ اللهَ وَهُوَ خادِعُهُمْ** (The hypocrites indeed seek to deceive Allah, but it is He who outwits them...) [Qarai 4:142].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِسُورٍ لَهُ بابٌ باطِنُهُ فِيهِ الرَّحْمَةُ وَظاهِرُهُ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ الْعَذابُ** (...Then a wall will be set up between them, with a door in it: its inside contains mercy and its outside is toward punishment) [Qarai 57:13]. The "wall" (السور) of a city is its barrier that separates its interior from its exterior. The pronoun in "a wall will be set up between them" refers to both the believers and the hypocrites; that is, a barrier wall will be set up between the believers and the hypocrites, separating one group from the other.

It has been said: The wall is the Elevations (الأعراف), which is not far-fetched, and an indication of this was previously mentioned in the interpretation of His saying: **وَبَيْنَهُما حِجابٌ وَعَلَى الْأَعْرافِ رِجالٌ** (And there will be a veil between them. And on the Elevations will be certain men...) [Qarai 7:46]. It has also been said that the wall is other than the Elevations.

His saying: **لَهُ بابٌ** (with a door in it); that is, the wall has a door. This resembles the state of the hypocrites in the world, for they were among the believers, having connection and association with them, yet they were nonetheless veiled from them by a barrier. Furthermore, they see the people of Paradise, which increases their regret and remorse.

His saying: **باطِنُهُ فِيهِ الرَّحْمَةُ وَظاهِرُهُ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ الْعَذابُ** (its inside contains mercy and its outside is toward punishment). "Its inside" (باطنه) is a subject, and the phrase "contains mercy" (فيه الرحمة) is a subject and predicate forming the news (خبر) for "its inside." Likewise, "its outside" (ظاهره) is a subject, and the phrase "is toward punishment" (من قبله العذاب) is a subject and predicate forming its news. The pronouns in "contains" (فيه) and "toward" (من قبله) refer to the inside and the outside.

It appears from the fact that the inside of the wall contains mercy and its outside is toward punishment that the wall surrounds the believers, who are within it, while the hypocrites are outside of it.

In the fact that its interior, which faces the believers, contains mercy, and its exterior, which faces the hypocrites, contains punishment, there is a correspondence to the state of faith in the world. For faith is a blessing for the people of sincerity among the believers, in which they rejoice and find pleasure, while it is a punishment for the people of hypocrisy, who feel constrained by professing it and suffer because of it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُنادُونَهُمْ أَلَمْ نَكُنْ مَعَكُمْ** (They will call out to them, ‘Were we not with you?’...) [Qarai 57:14]. This is a new sentence (استئناف) serving as an answer to an implied question, as if it were asked: "What will the hypocrites, men and women, do after the wall is set up and they witness the punishment from its exterior?" So it was said: "They will call out to them," etc.

The meaning is: The hypocrites, men and women, will call out to the believing men and women, saying: "Were we not with you?" meaning their being with the believing men and women in the world in the outward appearance of the religion.

His saying: **قالُوا بَلى** (They will say, ‘Yes!’...) [Qarai 57:14] is the answer of the believing men and women to them. The meaning is: "They will say," i.e., the believing men and women will say in response to them, "Yes, you were with us in the world, **وَلَكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنْتُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (but you tempted yourselves) [Qarai 57:14]; that is, you tried and destroyed yourselves, **وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ** (and you awaited [misfortune for us]) [Qarai 57:14] regarding the religion and its people, **وَارْتَبْتُمْ** (and you were in doubt) [Qarai 57:14] concerning your religion, **وَغَرَّتْكُمُ الْأَمانِيُّ** (and [false] hopes deluded you) [Qarai 57:14]—among which was your hope that the light of the religion would be extinguished and its people would abandon it—**حَتَّى جاءَ أَمْرُ اللهِ** (until Allah’s edict arrived) [Qarai 57:14], which is death, **وَغَرَّكُمْ بِاللهِ الْغَرُورُ** (and the Deceiver deceived you concerning Allah) [Qarai 57:14]. The "Deceiver" (الغرور) with a *fatha* on the *ghayn* is Satan.

The verse—as you see—indicates that the hypocrites, men and women, seek help from the believing men and women against the darkness they are in, pleading that they were with them in the world. Then it indicates that the believing men and women answer that they were indeed with them, but their hearts did not correspond to their outward state, as they tempted themselves, awaited misfortune, doubted, were deluded by false hopes, and were deceived concerning Allah by the Deceiver. These wicked qualities are maladies of the heart; thus, their hearts were not sound (سليم), and nothing benefits on the Day of Resurrection except a sound heart. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ لَا يَنْفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** (the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart) [Qarai 26:88-89].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** (So today no ransom shall be taken from you or from the faithless...) [Qarai 57:15]. This is the completion of the speech of the believing men and women, addressing the hypocrites, men and women, and adding to them the disbelievers—those who were open in their disbelief—that they are hostages to their deeds, as the Exalted said: **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ** (Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned) [Qarai 74:38]. No ransom (فدية) shall be taken from them by which they might free themselves. Ransom is one of two ways by which one is released from being a hostage; the other is a helper who assists and saves, which they negated by saying: **مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ** (Your abode is the Fire...) [Qarai 57:15].

So His saying: **مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ هِيَ مَوْلاكُمْ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ** (Your abode is the Fire: it is your master, and it is an evil destination) [Qarai 57:15] negates any helper who might assist them and save them from the Fire, other than the Fire itself, as indicated by the restriction in His saying: "it is your master" (هي مولاكم). The "master" (المولى) is the helper, and the sentence is intended as mockery.

It is also possible that "master" (المولى) means the one who takes charge of one's affairs. For they used to seek their needs of food, drink, clothing, marriage, and housing from other than Allah, the Glorified, and the reality of that was the Fire. So today, their master is the Fire, and it is what prepares those things for them; thus, their food is from *Zaqqum*, their drink is from boiling water (*Hamim*), their clothing is from garments cut out of fire, their companions are the devils, and their abode is the Fire, as Allah, the Glorified, has informed in many verses of His speech.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated that Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Dala'il* recorded via Zayd ibn Aslam, from 'Ata' ibn Yasar, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the year of Hudaybiyyah. When we were at 'Usfan, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Soon there will come a people whose deeds will make you look down upon your own deeds.' We asked: 'Who are they, O Messenger of Allah? Are they the Quraysh?' He said: 'No, but they are the people of Yemen; they have the most tender hearts and the softest souls.' We asked: 'Are they better than us, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'If one of them had a mountain of gold and spent it, he would not reach the *mudd* (a measure) of one of you, nor even half of it. Indeed, this is the distinction between us and the people: **لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ** (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought...)'" [Qarai 57:10].

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through several chains with similar wording, and they include the verse. However, there is a difficulty in that the apparent context of the verses suggests they were revealed after the conquest—meaning either Hudaybiyyah or the Conquest of Mecca—so it would not apply to what was before the conquest.

In the same source, it is narrated that 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from 'Ikrimah, who said: "When this verse was revealed: **لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ** (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought...), Abu al-Dahdah said: 'By Allah, I shall spend today a spending by which I will catch up to those before me, and no one after me shall surpass me in it.' He then said: 'O Allah, everything that Abu al-Dahdah owns, half of it is for Allah,' until he even reached his sandal strap and said: 'And this too.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying: **يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ** (The day you will see the faithful men and the faithful women, with their light moving swiftly before them and on their right...), he said: "The light will be distributed among the people on the Day of Resurrection according to their faith. It will be distributed to the hypocrite such that his light will be at the big toe of his left foot. He will look at his light and then say to the believers: 'Stay in your places so that I may take a light from your light!' The believers will say to them: 'Go back and look for a light!' and a wall will be set up between them with a door. They will call out to the believers from behind the wall: **أَلَمْ نَكُنْ مَعَكُمْ قالُوا بَلى وَلكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنْتُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (Were we not with you? They will say, ‘Yes! But you tempted yourselves...’)." He said: "By sins." **وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ وَارْتَبْتُمْ** (and you awaited [misfortune for us] and you were in doubt). He said: "Meaning you doubted and awaited misfortune."

Regarding His saying: **فَالْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ** (So today no ransom shall be taken from you...), he said: "By Allah, He did not mean the Jews or the Christians by that; He meant none but the people of the *Qiblah* (أهل القبلة)." Then He said: **مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ هِيَ مَوْلاكُمْ** (Your abode is the Fire: it is your master). He said: "It is more worthy of you (أولى بكم)."

**I say:** By "people of the *Qiblah*," he means the hypocrites among them.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Aban ibn Taghlib, who said: "I heard Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'Avoid false hopes (المنى), for they take away the splendor of what you have been granted, cause you to belittle the gifts of Allah (جل وعز) to you, and bring you regrets for what you have imagined for yourselves.'"
